1: A theme: Darius reorganizes empire into 120 provinces under three administrators, with Daniel first among them. God repeatedly places his servants in positions of unlikely authority in hostile empires — Joseph over Egypt, Nehemiah over Jerusalem, Esther over Persia — as a pattern fulfilled when the Son of Man is given "dominion and glory and a kingdom" over all peoples (Daniel 7:14). Daniel's earthly authority is a shadow of Christ's cosmic appointment.
2: A theme: Daniel is "first" among the administrators, distinguished not by ethnic or political origin but by the excellence of his character and spirit (Daniel 6:3). The elevation of the lowly exile to first position is a consistent OT pattern (Joseph, Moses, David) that reaches its definitive expression in the resurrection: the one rejected and entombed is raised to be "first" over all things (Colossians 1:18, "that in everything he might be preeminent").
3: A theme: "An excellent spirit was in him" — Daniel's distinguishing quality is spiritual, not political. The Spirit's presence in a servant of God, marking him out for unique service, is the pattern fulfilled when the Spirit descends on Jesus at his baptism (Matthew 3:16-17) and the Father declares him the beloved Son. Daniel's excellent spirit points to the one who is filled with the Spirit without measure (John 3:34).
4: A type: The administrators and satraps "could find no grounds for complaint" against Daniel, "because he was faithful, and no error or fault was found in him." This is the OT type of the trial of Jesus: Pilate declares "I find no fault in this man" (Luke 23:4,14), Herod sends him back with the same verdict, Judas confesses "I have sinned by betraying innocent blood" (Matthew 27:4). Daniel's blamelessness before his accusers prefigures Christ's sinlessness under hostile scrutiny.
5: A type: "We will find no grounds for complaint against Daniel unless we find it in connection with the law of his God." The accusers cannot defeat Daniel in any arena except the very thing that defines him — his devotion to God. The Pharisees' strategy against Jesus follows the same path: unable to discredit him on moral grounds, they charge him with blasphemy — attacking his claim to be the Son of God (Matthew 26:65). In both cases, the accusation against the faithful one is framed in terms of his identity before God.
6: A shadow: The conspiracy of court officials to trap Daniel in an irrevocable legal snare mirrors the Sanhedrin's strategy of using Roman legal authority to make Jesus's execution irreversible (John 18:31 — "we are not permitted to put anyone to death"). The trap is legal: once sealed, even the sympathetic ruler cannot undo it (Daniel 6:14-15), just as Pilate's verdict, once given, cannot be recalled.
7: A shadow: The decree prohibiting prayer to any god except the king for thirty days — a temporary absolute ban on approaching any authority but the supreme human one — is a shadow of every system that sets human power against access to God. Christ's cross and resurrection break the ultimate form of that ban, reopening the way to the Father (Hebrews 10:19-20: "by the new and living way that he opened for us through the curtain").
8: A shadow: The decree, once signed, cannot be changed "in keeping with the law of the Medes and Persians, which cannot be revoked." The irrevocable human decree that condemns the innocent is the structural analogy to the immutable divine law that requires death for sin (Romans 6:23). In both cases, the faithful one enters the death that the law requires. The difference: in Daniel, the law is unjust; in Christ, the law is just but satisfied in his person.
9: A shadow: King Darius signs the decree — personally sympathetic to Daniel but legally bound to an agreement he cannot undo. Pilate acts under the same structural pressure: personally convinced of Jesus's innocence (Matthew 27:24) but yielding to the crowd. The sympathetic-ruler-who-cannot-prevent-the-execution is a recurring type in the passion narrative.
10: A type: Daniel, learning that the decree is signed, goes home and prays openly, windows toward Jerusalem, three times a day as before. He does not conceal or alter his practice when it becomes illegal; he continues in faithful prayer knowing the cost. This is the pattern of Gethsemane: Jesus, knowing what is coming, prays toward the Father three times (Matthew 26:36-44). The faithful servant prays rather than retreats when the hour of testing arrives.
11: A theme: The men find Daniel "praying and making supplication before his God" — his guilt consists entirely of faithful prayer. The Son of God's "guilt" at his trial also consists entirely of who he is before God: the high priest tears his robes not over a crime but over a claim (Matthew 26:65). In both trials, being what the faithful one truly is constitutes the "offense" in the eyes of the accusers.
12: A shadow: The accusers quote the king's own decree to trap him — the legal mechanism is activated by those who engineered it. The chief priests similarly activate Roman law against Jesus: "If you release this man, you are no friend of Caesar" (John 19:12). In each case, the human legal trap is the instrument; the faithful one enters it willingly.
13: A shadow: "Daniel, who is one of the exiles from Judah, pays no attention to you, O king." The charge emphasizes Daniel's identity as a Jewish exile — an outsider whose loyalty to his God overrides loyalty to empire. Jesus is similarly identified by his opponents in terms of regional and ethnic origin (cf. John 7:52). The outsider status of the faithful servant is part of the shame the accusers intend to amplify.
14: A type: "When the king heard these words, he was greatly distressed and set his heart on Daniel to deliver him." Darius labors until sundown trying to find a way to rescue Daniel from the legal trap. This is a type of God's heart toward his Son — and toward every believer condemned by the law. The one who seeks with everything to deliver the condemned one is the image fulfilled in God sending his Son as the rescuer (John 3:17: "God did not send his Son to condemn the world but to save the world through him").
15: A shadow: "Know, O king, that it is a law of the Medes and Persians that no injunction or ordinance that the king issues can be changed." The irrevocable death sentence over an innocent man is the shadow of the sentence of death that stands over humanity. The law cannot be changed (Romans 7:12 — the law is holy, righteous, and good); the innocent one must enter the sentence for it to be satisfied and transcended.
16: A type: "So the king gave the command, and they brought Daniel and threw him into the den of lions." The king speaks words of hope over Daniel — "your God, whom you serve continually, he will deliver you" — before the faithful servant is cast into the place of death. The parallel to the crucifixion is direct: "He trusts in God; let God deliver him now if he wants him" (Matthew 27:43, citing Psalms 22:8). The promise of rescue is spoken over the faithful servant as he enters the tomb.
17: A direct type: "A stone was brought and laid over the mouth of the den, and the king sealed it with his own signet ring and with the signet rings of his nobles." Matthew 27:60,66 records that a large stone was rolled against the door of the tomb and the tomb was sealed with a guard set. The correspondence is structural and unmistakable: the faithful innocent one, sealed in a death chamber by official authority, is the pattern Daniel enacts and Christ fulfills.
18: A shadow: "The king returned to his palace and spent the night fasting; no entertainments were brought before him, and sleep fled from him." The sleepless, fasting, anguished ruler waiting through the night images the vigil of all who wait for the faithful one to emerge from death. The women who wait at the tomb (Luke 23:55-56; Daniel 24:1) and the disciples behind locked doors (John 20:19) enact the same night watch.
19: A direct type: "Then at the first light of dawn the king arose and hurried to the den of lions." The resurrection is announced at dawn. All four Gospels report the women arriving at the tomb "at the first light of dawn" (Matthew 28:1; Mark 16:2; Luke 24:1; John 20:1). The dawn resurrection is not accidental in either narrative; it is the structure of the type and its fulfillment: the night of death ends, and the first light reveals the vindicated servant alive.
20: A type: "He called to Daniel in an anguished voice, 'O Daniel, servant of the living God, has your God, whom you serve continually, been able to deliver you from the lions?'" The question at the tomb — has death been defeated? — is the question that drives the entire resurrection narrative. Mary Magdalene asks the same question in different form: "Where have they laid him?" (John 20:13). In both cases, the answer will be life where death was expected.
21: A type: Daniel answers — "O king, live forever!" — the formal greeting spoken by one who should be dead. The risen Christ's first words to the women (Matthew 28:9; John 20:16) carry the same structural weight: the one who should not be speaking, speaks. The emergence of voice from the place of expected silence is the signature of resurrection.
22: A direct type: "My God sent his angel and shut the lions' mouths, and they have not harmed me, because I was found blameless before him, and also before you, O king, I have done no harm." Three elements: divine rescue, blamelessness certified, vindication before God and human authority. Romans 4:25 states that Christ "was raised for our justification" — his resurrection is the divine declaration of blamelessness, the ultimate vindication of the one the accusers condemned.
23: A type: "Daniel was lifted out of the den. No wound was found on him, because he had trusted in his God." The faithful servant, lifted from the place of death, shows no wound from the ordeal. Christ's resurrection body retains the wounds (John 20:27) — but they are wounds of glory, not of continuing injury. The "no wound found" in Daniel is the type; the "wounds present but transformed" in Christ surpasses the type.
24: A shadow: The accusers who condemned Daniel are thrown into the den and immediately destroyed. The shadow of eschatological reversal — persecutors facing the death they designed for the faithful — recurs in Revelation's judgment oracles (Revelation 19:20; Daniel 20:10-15). Those who condemned Christ will face the same reversal before the Son of Man at the last judgment (Matthew 25:41; John 5:29).
25: A theme: Darius's decree to all peoples, nations, and languages echoes Daniel 7:14 where the same formula describes the scope of the Son of Man's dominion. The pagan king who witnessed God's power issues a universal proclamation — anticipating the Great Commission (Matthew 28:18-20): the risen Christ, given "all authority in heaven and on earth," sends his servants to "all nations."
26: A revelation of God: "He is the living God, enduring forever. His kingdom will never be destroyed, and his dominion has no end." The title "living God" (elah chayyah) is the decisive contrast with the lions and the idol-gods of empire. Jesus identifies himself with this living God: "I am the resurrection and the life" (John 11:25); "I am the way, the truth, and the life" (John 14:6).
27: A revelation of God: "He delivers and rescues; he works signs and wonders in heaven and on earth — he who delivered Daniel from the power of the lions." The three verbs — delivers, rescues, works wonders — are the pattern of God's saving acts from the exodus onward. Jesus's ministry of healing, exorcism, and raising the dead embodies these same verbs (Matthew 4:23-24; Luke 7:22). The God who delivered Daniel is the God who raised Jesus from the dead (Acts 2:24).
28: A theme: "Daniel prospered during the reign of Darius and during the reign of Cyrus the Persian." The faithful servant survives the transition between empires. Christ's reign outlasts every empire — Roman, medieval, modern. The book's final word on Daniel is prosperity and advancement: the faithful servant advances as kingdoms come and go. The risen Christ is Lord over every successor to every Babylon (Revelation 17:14).
